retrieved_docs.title: Hypertension Clinical Trials  -- Issues of Concern -- Exclusion Criteria
retrieved_docs.content: Angina in the prior three months Symptomatic heart failure or left ventricular ejection fraction <40% Acute coronary syndrome or revascularization in the prior month Cerebrovascular disease or transient ischemic attack in the previous three months Severe or refractory hypertension

retrieved_docs.title: Ranolazine -- Indications -- FDA-Approved Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Chronic angina

retrieved_docs.title: Angina -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Angina, or chest pain, is the most common symptom of ischemic heart disease, a major cause of morbidity and mortality worldwide. Chest pain can be due to non-cardiac and cardiac causes, and thorough history and physical is critical in differentiating these causes and identifying patients experiencing acute coronary syndrome. Angina is one of the signs of acute coronary syndrome (ACS) and can further subdivide into stable and unstable angina. Stable angina defines as the occurrence of symptoms with exertion only. Unstable angina or symptoms occurring at rest requires more prompt evaluation and management. Approximately 9 million patients in the United States have symptoms of angina, and recognizing these symptoms is imperative in improving patient outcomes. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Chronic Ischemic Heart Disease Selection of Treatment Modality -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Clinically, if all three criteria are met (substernal chest pain, occurring with exertion and emotion, relieved by rest or nitroglycerin), then it is considered typical angina. This definition is meant to imply a high likelihood of ischemic heart disease as the cause of chest discomfort in the right clinical context. Frequently, patients will not fulfill all three criteria and will be further stratified into atypical angina or non-cardiac chest pain, depending on how many criteria they meet and the overall description of their symptoms.

retrieved_docs.title: Ranolazine -- Indications -- Clinical Evidence
retrieved_docs.content: The Combination Assessment of Ranolazine in Stable Angina (CARISA) trial was a multinational, randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled trial of patients with symptomatic chronic angina despite taking standard doses of diltiazem, atenolol, or amlodipine. [6] Patients were randomly assigned to receive a placebo or 750 to 1000 mg of ranolazine twice daily. Outcome measures included a change in exercise time, time to onset of symptoms, time to onset of ischemia, need for nitroglycerin, and the number of angina attacks. Exercise duration, time to angina symptoms, and time to ischemia increased more from baseline in both ranolazine groups compared to the placebo group. Exercise tolerance increased with ranolazine dose and plasma concentration. These changes were independent of heart rate, blood pressure, or background antianginal therapy. Angina attacks and nitroglycerin use were reduced by about 7 days in the ranolazine group compared to placebo.

retrieved_docs.title: Chronic Total Occlusion of the Coronary Artery -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: A "true" CTO is defined as 100% occlusion of a coronary artery with TIMI 0 flow; whereas a "functional" CTO is defined as severely stenotic, yet, less than a 100% occlusion of the coronary artery with TIMI 1 flow for a duration of greater than or equal to 3 months based on angiographic evidence. [2] Moreover, it is difficult to identify the exact period over which a CTO lesion is present in the absence of serial angiograms. Therefore, it is mostly estimated based on available clinical information related to the timing of the event that caused the occlusion, for example, prior myocardial infarction or sudden change in anginal symptoms with electrocardiogram changes consistent with the location of the occlusion. However, in many patients, the age of the CTO cannot be determined with confidence.

retrieved_docs.title: TIMI Grade Flow -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Acute myocardial infarction (MI) is the leading cause of death worldwide. It has been widely accepted that it is due to the insufficient blood supply to the cardiac tissue. In an attempt to intervene, physicians in the 1970s studied ligation of canine coronary arteries and documented prevention of necrosis with ligation release under appropriate time limits. Initial therapeutic reperfusion was established using intravenous and intracoronary thrombolytic agents, and several studies were conducted on them. [1] [2] [3] [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Coronary Perfusion Pressure -- Definition/Introduction -- Overview
retrieved_docs.content: Myocardial oxygen extraction is the highest of any organ at approximately 70% to 80% under resting conditions, leaving little reserve. CPP impairment can lead to ischemia, myocardial dysfunction, and even irreversible damage, particularly in the presence of stress or cardiovascular diseases. Adequate CPP ensures sufficient oxygen delivery, especially when demand increases, such as during exercise or a tachycardic state. Patients with acute coronary syndrome (ACS) or heart failure must maintain adequate CPP to sustain myocardial function during acute stress. Prolonged reductions in CPP can cause myocardial stunning, infarction, or cardiogenic shock, creating a cycle that further reduces CPP. Preserving the CPP is also vital in ACS to limit infarct size and ensure perfusion to viable myocardium. [1] [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Posterior Myocardial Infarction -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: A thorough history, including onset, duration, provoking, and relieving factors, quality, and associated factors is critical. Posterior myocardial infarction, like other types of myocardial infarction, classically presents with chest pain. Patients often describe the pain as crushing substernal chest pain or pressure. Many patients insist it is only pressure and will not describe it as pain or use phrases like "an elephant sitting on my chest." They may have radiation to the arms, jaw, or epigastrium. The sensation may be relieved with nitroglycerin. While not a definitive way to differentiate chest pain of cardiac origin from chest pain from other causes, there are factors that increase and others that decrease the likelihood of ACS. Factors that increase the probability of ACS include: associated with nausea or vomiting associated with diaphoresis is worse with exertion or strong emotion Chest pain that radiates to the arms or jaw Factors that decrease the probability of ACS include:

retrieved_docs.title: Drug Eluting Stent Compounds -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Problems with hypersensitivity to stents have been proposed and subsequently, the concept of polymer-free or biodegradable polymers was introduced. Recently reestablishment of healthy long-term vasomotion with biodegradable stents have gained significance.

retrieved_docs.title: Amlodipine -- Indications -- FDA-Approved Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Hypertension:  Amlodipine is an excellent first-line choice among the multiple options of antihypertensive agents. It may be used alone or in combination with other antihypertensive agents. [1] [2] Chronic stable angina: Amlodipine is indicated for the symptomatic treatment of chronic stable angina. Amlodipine may be used alone or in combination with other antianginal agents. [3] Vasospastic angina (Prinzmetal or variant angina): Amlodipine is indicated to treat confirmed or suspected vasospastic angina. [4] Angiographically documented coronary artery disease: In patients with recently documented coronary artery disease by angiography and without heart failure or an ejection fraction <40%, amlodipine is indicated to reduce the risk of hospitalization from angina and reduce the risk of a coronary revascularization procedure. [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Captopril -- Indications -- Raynaud Phenomenon
retrieved_docs.content: The Raynaud phenomenon is diagnosed clinically, and a cold stimulation test can precipitate an attack. Labs are needed only if another condition is suspected. Treatment involves avoiding colds and smoking cessation. Medical management consists of dihydropyridine-type calcium channel blockers (eg, nifedipine or nimodipine) and phosphodiesterase inhibitors. Some studies have highlighted that captopril decreases the frequency and severity of ischemic attacks in patients with Raynaud disease but not in patients with scleroderma. However, more definitive studies are needed to elucidate its effects. [7] A clinical trial evaluated patients for up to 3 months; additional studies are necessary to determine full effectiveness. [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Troponin -- Etiology and Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: Troponin measurements were found to have a near 100% sensitivity when checked 6 to 12 hours after the start of chest pain and have a significantly improved specificity for cardiac muscle damage compared to previous biomarkers. [9] Due to its clinical usefulness, serial troponin testing was added to the Fourth Universal Definition of Myocardial Infarction, the current definition used by the American College of Cardiology. [15]

retrieved_docs.title: Atenolol -- Indications -- FDA-Approved Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Atenolol is approved by the U.S. Food and Drug Administration (FDA) for treating hypertension, angina pectoris, and acute myocardial infarction.

retrieved_docs.title: Stable Angina -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Stable angina, also known as typical angina or angina pectoris, is a symptom of myocardial ischemia. Stable angina is characterized by chest discomfort or anginal equivalent that is provoked with exertion and alleviated at rest or with nitroglycerin. This is often 1 of the first manifestations or warning signs of underlying coronary disease. Angina affects 10 million people in the United States (US); given this, it is important to recognize the signs and symptoms and appropriately risk stratify and manage these individuals. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Cardiac Syndrome X -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Although once believed to be a benign ailment, contemporary literature has supported subjects with CSX to have unfavorable cardiovascular events and a decreased quality of life. It is a diagnosis of exclusion and is frequently challenging. Management is also complex and difficult as the pathogenesis of its onset is not fully understood. CSX is hypothesized to involve microvascular dysfunction of the coronary arteries, resulting in myocardial ischemia (MI) and irregular cardiac sensitivity to pain.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 4 -- Community 2908
retrieved_docs.content: Other and ill-defined heart disease is synonymous with تمدد الأوعية الدموية في القلب, a term that describes dilation of the blood vessels within the heart. This equivalence indicates that the ambiguous classification of certain cardiac conditions corresponds to a pathological enlargement of the coronary or myocardial vasculature, which can affect cardiac function and warrants appropriate diagnostic evaluation and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 2 -- Community 711
retrieved_docs.content: atherosclerosis and other heart disease, from other and ill‑defined heart disease, and from داء تصلب الشرايين القلبي الوعائي (cardiovascular atherosclerotic disease). The term other and ill‑defined heart disease is synonymous with تمدد الأوعية الدموية في القلب (vascular dilation in the heart), providing an alternative description for this ambiguous cardiac condition. Overall, these relationships illustrate a comprehensive network in which pericardial disease (مرض تأموري) encompasses structural, inflammatory, and procedural entities; coronary atherosclerotic pathology is intertwined with inflammatory morphologies and pericarditis; and specific clinical entities such as ischemic heart disease without angina are linked to broader atherosclerotic and ill‑defined cardiac diagnoses, all of which have implications for diagnosis, classification, and therapeutic planning.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 1973
retrieved_docs.content: Symptoms are closely related to, and may be considered synonymous with, the signs and symptoms of ill‑defined conditions, reflecting the overlap between patient‑reported experiences and observable clinical manifestations in poorly characterized disorders. In this framework, diseases and injuries are placed under the broader category of signs and symptoms of ill‑defined conditions, indicating that many inadequately defined pathologies present with nonspecific clinical findings that are captured by this umbrella term. Together, these relationships underscore the interconnected nature of clinical presentations (symptoms) and the underlying, often vague, disease or injury processes, highlighting the importance of comprehensive evaluation when managing patients with ill‑defined conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1826
retrieved_docs.content: The category “signs and symptoms of ill‑defined conditions” is presented as a parent concept that includes the specific child entity “غشي.” In this framework, the general term “Symptoms” is described as closely related to, and potentially synonymous with, the broader phrase “signs and symptoms of ill‑defined conditions,” indicating that the two expressions refer to overlapping clinical manifestations. Consequently, the specific manifestation “غشي” is understood to fall under the umbrella of nonspecific or ill‑defined symptomatology, and clinicians should recognize its inclusion within this broader symptom category when evaluating and documenting patient presentations.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 2 -- Community 1242
retrieved_docs.content: Symptoms are closely related to and may be considered synonymous with the broader category of signs and symptoms of ill‑defined conditions. Within this group of nonspecific clinical manifestations, the specific finding of الانهيار في الدورة الدموية (circulatory collapse) is recognized as a child element, illustrating that severe cardiovascular failure can be one of the many possible presentations encompassed by ill‑defined symptom complexes. This relationship underscores the importance of viewing individual symptoms as part of a larger, sometimes vague, clinical picture when assessing patients with ambiguous or poorly characterized disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 1 -- Community 90
retrieved_docs.content: delineate a network of adrenergic antagonists, peripheral α‑blockers, and antiarrhythmic agents, their hierarchical classifications, receptor targets, chemical taxonomy, physiologic effects, and clinically relevant contraindications, providing clinicians with an integrated framework for therapeutic selection and patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2578
retrieved_docs.content: Acute and unspecified renal failure is described as being related to and possibly synonymous with كلاء الكليون السفلي, indicating that the two terms may refer to the same clinical concept.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 3 -- Community 1771
retrieved_docs.content: Symptoms are closely related to and may be considered synonymous with the broader category of signs and symptoms of ill‑defined conditions. In this hierarchical framework, diseases and injuries are classified as having the parent category of signs and symptoms of ill‑defined conditions, indicating that they are understood through the manifestation of such nonspecific signs. A specific example of a child element within this category is الانهيار في الدورة الدموية (circulatory collapse), which represents a distinct clinical manifestation that can arise from ill‑defined conditions. Together, these relationships highlight that the identification of symptoms and signs, including serious events like circulatory collapse, is essential for recognizing and managing diseases and injuries that present without clear, well‑defined diagnostic criteria.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 4 -- Community 2907
retrieved_docs.content: Ischemische hartziekte zonder angina is understood as a specific manifestation that is mapped from the broader category other and ill-defined heart disease. The term other and ill-defined heart disease is synonymous with the Arabic phrase تمدد الأوعية الدموية في القلب, which describes dilation of the blood vessels in the heart. Consequently, Ischemische hartziekte zonder angina, other and ill-defined heart disease, and تمدد الأوعية الدموية في القلب are interconnected concepts representing a form of heart disease characterized by vascular changes without typical anginal symptoms.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 3 -- Community 2675
retrieved_docs.content: Symptoms are closely related to, and may be considered synonymous with, the signs and symptoms of ill‑defined conditions, reflecting an overlap in terminology where both terms describe the observable manifestations and patient‑reported experiences associated with poorly characterized medical disorders. This conceptual equivalence highlights the importance of recognizing that “symptoms” and “signs and symptoms of ill‑defined conditions” often refer to the same clinical features, facilitating clearer communication in diagnosis and patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 3 -- Community 1975
retrieved_docs.content: Tirofiban and its formulation tirofiban hydrochlorid are reported to have a preventive effect on إقفار عَضَلِ القَلْبِ. إقفار عَضَلِ القَلْبِ encompasses the more specific condition احْتِشاءُ عَضَلِ القَلْبِ الحادّ للجدار الأمامي, indicating that acute anterior‑wall myocardial infarction is a child concept of this broader cardiac event. In the hierarchical relationship, إقفار عَضَلِ القَلْبِ is considered a form of اعتلال عضلة القلب, linking the acute occlusive process to the larger category of cardiomyopathy. Clinically, إقفار عَضَلِ القَلْبِ frequently co‑occurs with خفقانات, reflecting the common presentation of palpitations in patients experiencing this cardiac condition.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 1533
retrieved_docs.content: Tirofiban and tirofiban hydrochlorid may prevent إقفار عَضَلِ القَلْبِ. إقفار عَضَلِ القَلْبِ is understood as a specific manifestation of اعتلال عضلة القلب and it includes تمدد الأوعية الدموية في القلب as a subordinate condition; تمدد الأوعية الدموية في القلب is synonymous with the category “other and ill-defined heart disease.” Additionally, خفقانات frequently co‑occur with إقفار عَضَلِ القَلْبِ. Recognizing that tirofiban agents can prevent this cardiac muscle arrest, that it is a form of cardiomyopathy associated with vascular dilatation in the heart, and that palpitations often accompany it, informs clinical assessment and therapeutic decision‑making.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1786
retrieved_docs.content: Tirofiban and tirofiban hydrochlorid may prevent إقفار عَضَلِ القَلْبِ, a condition that is classified under the broader category of vascular ischemia. Fingolimod is contraindicated in patients who have إقفار عَضَلِ القَلْبِ. Clinical observations indicate that إقفار عَضَلِ القَلْبِ frequently co‑occurs with خفقانات, and the acute event can evolve into مرض قلبي إقفاري مزمن، غير محدد, a chronic unspecified ischemic heart disease.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 4 -- Community 2819
retrieved_docs.content: Acute myocardial infarction (احْتِشاءُ عَضَلِ القَلْبِ الحادّ) is closely related and may be considered synonymous with coronary artery occlusion (انسداد الشريان التاجي). It is also mapped to coronary artery embolism (انصمام الشريان التاجي), indicating that these terms describe similar pathological events in which a blockage of a coronary artery—whether by occlusion or embolic material—leads to sudden injury of the heart muscle. Recognizing the interchangeability of these concepts assists clinicians in diagnosing and managing the acute coronary syndrome that results from compromised coronary blood flow.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 4 -- Community 2914
retrieved_docs.content: Chest pain (ألم الصَدْر) is clinically associated with cardiac catheterization (قسطرة قلبية). In clinical practice, the presence of chest pain often prompts consideration of cardiac catheterization to evaluate coronary artery disease, while the procedure itself may produce chest discomfort as a post‑procedural symptom. Recognizing this association helps clinicians diagnose underlying cardiac pathology and manage patient symptoms before, during, and after the intervention.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 4 -- Community 2908
retrieved_docs.content: Ischemic heart disease (إقفار عَضَلِ القَلْبِ) is recognized as a specific form of myocardial disease (اعتلال عضلة القلب). It serves as a broader category that includes subconditions such as Myo ENDO PERICARDIAL & VALVE DISORDERS and vascular ischemia, both of which are described as having ischemic heart disease as their parent. Clinically, ischemic heart disease often co‑occurs with palpitations (خفقانات) and encompasses a chronic, unspecified form known as مرض قلبي إقفاري مزمن، غير محدد. Preventive pharmacotherapy may involve the antiplatelet agents tirofiban and tirofiban hydrochlorid, both of which are indicated as potentially preventing the development of ischemic heart disease. This integrated understanding highlights the hierarchical classification, associated symptoms, and preventive options relevant to patient care.
